expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to naming and writing formulas for binary ionic compounds with fixed-charge metals (Type I), where the metal always forms the same charge, enabling straightforward naming without Roman numerals.

core_concept.principle: Type I binary ionic compounds contain a metal with a fixed charge (Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, etc.) and a nonmetal; the name follows the pattern [metal name] [nonmetal root]-ide without Roman numerals.
core_concept.explanation: Type I ionic compounds are the simplest to name because the metal always forms the same charge. Group 1 metals (alkali metals) always form +1 ions; Group 2 metals (alkaline earth metals) always form +2 ions; aluminum always forms +3; zinc always forms +2; silver always forms +1. Since the charge is predictable, we don't need Roman numerals in the name. The naming pattern is straightforward: name the metal unchanged, then name the nonmetal with the -ide suffix. For example, NaCl is sodium chloride (not sodium(I) chloride), and MgO is magnesium oxide (not magnesium(II) oxide). This simplicity makes Type I compounds the foundation for learning ionic nomenclature before progressing to Type II compounds with variable-charge metals.

image.alt: Diagram showing Type I ionic compound naming pattern with examples
image.caption: Type I ionic naming: metal name + nonmetal root + -ide (no Roman numerals needed).

sections.section_title: Identifying Type I Metals: Fixed-Charge Elements
sections.introduction: Before naming Type I ionic compounds, you must be able to identify which metals have fixed charges. These metals are predictable and don't require Roman numerals in their names.

key_concept.definition: Type I metals are elements that always form the same ionic charge: Group 1 (+1), Group 2 (+2), Al (+3), Zn (+2), Ag (+1), Cd (+2), and a few others. Their charges are determined by their position in the periodic table.
key_concept.context: These metals are predictable because they lose all valence electrons to achieve noble gas configuration. Group 1 loses 1 electron (+1), Group 2 loses 2 electrons (+2), Al loses 3 electrons (+3). Zn, Ag, and Cd are exceptions that also have fixed charges despite being transition metals.

classifications.value: Always form +1 ions (Li⁺, Na⁺, K⁺, Rb⁺, Cs⁺, Fr⁺)
characteristics: Lose 1 electron to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: All Group 1 metals form +1 ions exclusively; never see Na²⁺ or K³⁺ in normal chemistry.

examples.explanation: Na (Group 1) → Na⁺ + e⁻. Sodium always forms +1 ion. Name: sodium (not sodium(I)). Compound: NaCl = sodium chloride.

image.alt: Sodium atom losing electron to form Na⁺
image.caption: Group 1 metals: always lose 1 electron → +1 charge.

examples.explanation: K (Group 1) → K⁺. Compounds: KCl (potassium chloride), K₂O (potassium oxide), K₃N (potassium nitride). Never potassium(I).

image.caption: Potassium always +1: no Roman numeral needed.

classifications.value: Always form +2 ions (Be²⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Sr²⁺, Ba²⁺, Ra²⁺)
characteristics: Lose 2 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: All Group 2 metals form +2 ions exclusively; never see Mg⁺ or Ca³⁺ in normal chemistry.

examples.explanation: Mg (Group 2) → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻. Magnesium always forms +2 ion. Name: magnesium (not magnesium(II)). Compound: MgO = magnesium oxide.

image.alt: Magnesium atom losing 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺
image.caption: Group 2 metals: always lose 2 electrons → +2 charge.

examples.explanation: Ca (Group 2) → Ca²⁺. Compounds: CaCl₂ (calcium chloride), CaO (calcium oxide), Ca₃N₂ (calcium nitride). Never calcium(II).

image.caption: Calcium always +2: no Roman numeral needed.

characteristics: Loses 3 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
Only metal in Group 13 with fixed charge
classifications.behavior: Aluminum exclusively forms +3 ions; Al⁺ and Al²⁺ don't exist in normal chemistry.

examples.explanation: Al (Group 13) → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻. Aluminum always forms +3 ion. Name: aluminum (not aluminum(III)). Compound: Al₂O₃ = aluminum oxide.

image.alt: Aluminum atom losing 3 electrons to form Al³⁺
image.caption: Aluminum: always loses 3 electrons → +3 charge.

examples.explanation: Al³⁺ compounds: AlCl₃ (aluminum chloride), Al₂O₃ (aluminum oxide), AlN (aluminum nitride). Never aluminum(III).

image.caption: Aluminum always +3: no Roman numeral needed.

classifications.value: Zn (+2), Ag (+1), Cd (+2) - exceptions among transition metals
classifications.behavior: Unlike most transition metals, Zn, Ag, and Cd have only one common oxidation state, so no Roman numerals needed.

examples.explanation: Zn always forms Zn²⁺. Compounds: ZnCl₂ (zinc chloride), ZnO (zinc oxide), ZnS (zinc sulfide). Never zinc(II).

image.caption: Zinc: transition metal but always +2, no Roman numeral.

examples.explanation: Ag always forms Ag⁺. Compounds: AgCl (silver chloride), AgNO₃ (silver nitrate), Ag₂O (silver oxide). Never silver(I).

image.caption: Silver: transition metal but always +1, no Roman numeral.

examples.explanation: Cd always forms Cd²⁺. Compounds: CdCl₂ (cadmium chloride), CdS (cadmium sulfide). Never cadmium(II).

image.caption: Cadmium: transition metal but always +2, no Roman numeral.

principles.statement: For Type I metals, the group number directly predicts the ionic charge: Group 1 = +1, Group 2 = +2, Group 13 (Al) = +3.
principles.application: Enables charge prediction without memorization for Groups 1, 2, and Al; Zn, Ag, Cd must be memorized as exceptions.
principles.example: Na (Group 1) → Na⁺; Mg (Group 2) → Mg²⁺; Al (Group 13) → Al³⁺. Group number = charge for these metals.
principles.analogy: Like a uniform: Group 1 wears '+1 badge', Group 2 wears '+2 badge'—always the same, never changes.

image.alt: Periodic table highlighting Type I metals with their fixed charges
image.caption: Type I metal charges: Group 1 (+1), Group 2 (+2), Al (+3), Zn (+2), Ag (+1), Cd (+2).

principles.principle: No Roman Numerals for Type I
principles.statement: Type I metals never use Roman numerals in names because their charge is fixed and predictable.
principles.application: Distinguishes Type I from Type II naming: 'sodium chloride' not 'sodium(I) chloride'; 'magnesium oxide' not 'magnesium(II) oxide'.
principles.example: Correct: sodium chloride, magnesium oxide, aluminum oxide. Incorrect: sodium(I) chloride, magnesium(II) oxide, aluminum(III) oxide.
principles.analogy: Like a first name only: if everyone knows you have only one identity (fixed charge), you don't need to specify which one (Roman numeral).

image.alt: Comparison showing correct vs. incorrect Type I naming
image.caption: Type I naming: never use Roman numerals; charge is understood from metal identity.

principles.statement: Ionic compound formulas are determined by balancing total positive and negative charges to zero, not by prefixes.
principles.application: Predicts formulas: Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl (1:1); Mg²⁺ + Cl⁻ → MgCl₂ (1:2); Al³⁺ + O²⁻ → Al₂O₃ (2:3).
principles.example: Mg²⁺ + Cl⁻: Need 2 Cl⁻ to balance 1 Mg²⁺ (+2 + 2(-1) = 0) → MgCl₂. No prefixes needed; charge balance determines ratio.
principles.analogy: Like a seesaw: total weight on each side must balance; charges must sum to zero for neutral compound.

image.alt: Diagram showing charge balance determining ionic formulas
image.caption: Charge balance: total positive charge must equal total negative charge for neutral compound.

components.definition: Type I metal identification and naming rules
components.interpretation: Metal name unchanged (no Roman numeral); nonmetal name changed to end in -ide; formula from charge balance.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Type I Metals from Formula
worked_examples.problem: For each compound, identify if the metal is Type I (fixed charge): (a) NaCl, (b) FeCl₂, (c) MgO, (d) CuSO₄, (e) Al₂O₃, (f) ZnCl₂.

step_by_step.calculation: Na is Group 1 → always +1 → Type I ✓
Fe is transition metal (not Zn, Ag, Cd) → variable charge → Type II ✗
Mg is Group 2 → always +2 → Type I ✓
Cu is transition metal (not Zn, Ag, Cd) → variable charge → Type II ✗
Al is Group 13 → always +3 → Type I ✓
Zn is special transition metal → always +2 → Type I ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: Type I metals: (a) NaCl ✓ (Na, Group 1), (c) MgO ✓ (Mg, Group 2), (e) Al₂O₃ ✓ (Al, Group 13), (f) ZnCl₂ ✓ (Zn, special fixed-charge). Type II metals: (b) FeCl₂ ✗ (Fe, variable charge), (d) CuSO₄ ✗ (Cu, variable charge). Type I metals are Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd only.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying Type I vs. Type II metals from periodic position and exceptions

worked_examples.title: Predicting Ion Charges from Periodic Position
worked_examples.problem: Predict the ionic charge for each Type I metal: (a) potassium, (b) strontium, (c) aluminum, (d) silver, (e) zinc.

step_by_step.calculation: K is Group 1 → loses 1 electron → K⁺ (+1 charge)
Sr is Group 2 → loses 2 electrons → Sr²⁺ (+2 charge)
Al is Group 13 → loses 3 electrons → Al³⁺ (+3 charge)
Ag is special transition metal → always Ag⁺ (+1 charge, must memorize)
Zn is special transition metal → always Zn²⁺ (+2 charge, must memorize)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) K⁺ (+1, Group 1); (b) Sr²⁺ (+2, Group 2); (c) Al³⁺ (+3, Group 13); (d) Ag⁺ (+1, memorized exception); (e) Zn²⁺ (+2, memorized exception). Groups 1, 2, 13 follow group number pattern; Zn, Ag, Cd are memorized exceptions.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting Type I metal charges from periodic table position

sections.section_title: Naming Type I Binary Ionic Compounds
sections.introduction: Once you've identified a Type I metal, naming the compound follows a simple, consistent pattern. Master this pattern and you can name any Type I binary ionic compound.

key_concept.definition: Type I binary ionic compound naming: (1) Name the metal unchanged, (2) Name the nonmetal with -ide suffix, (3) No prefixes, no Roman numerals. Example: NaCl = sodium chloride.
key_concept.context: This pattern works for all Type I compounds regardless of the specific metal or nonmetal. The key is recognizing Type I metals and applying the consistent naming rule.

classifications.value: Metal name + nonmetal-ide (e.g., sodium chloride, potassium oxide)
characteristics: 1:1 or other ratios from charge balance
classifications.behavior: All Group 1 compounds follow same pattern; formula determined by charge balance (+1 with various anions).

examples.explanation: Na (sodium) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: sodium chloride. Formula: Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl (charges balance 1:1).

image.alt: NaCl naming breakdown showing metal and nonmetal parts
image.caption: NaCl: sodium (metal) + chloride (nonmetal-ide) = sodium chloride.

examples.explanation: K (potassium) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: potassium oxide. Formula: 2K⁺ + O²⁻ → K₂O (2(+1) + (-2) = 0).

image.caption: K₂O: potassium oxide (no prefix needed; charge balance determines 2:1 ratio).

examples.explanation: Li (lithium) + N (nitrogen → nitride). Name: lithium nitride. Formula: 3Li⁺ + N³⁻ → Li₃N (3(+1) + (-3) = 0).

image.caption: Li₃N: lithium nitride (charge balance determines 3:1 ratio).

classifications.value: Metal name + nonmetal-ide (e.g., magnesium chloride, calcium oxide)
characteristics: Ratios vary based on anion charge
classifications.behavior: All Group 2 compounds follow same pattern; +2 charge requires different ratios with different anions.

examples.explanation: Mg (magnesium) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: magnesium chloride. Formula: Mg²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → MgCl₂ (+2 + 2(-1) = 0).

image.caption: MgCl₂: magnesium chloride (no Roman numeral; Mg always +2).

examples.explanation: Ca (calcium) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: calcium oxide. Formula: Ca²⁺ + O²⁻ → CaO (+2 + (-2) = 0).

image.caption: CaO: calcium oxide (1:1 ratio, charges balance directly).

examples.explanation: Ba (barium) + N (nitrogen → nitride). Name: barium nitride. Formula: 3Ba²⁺ + 2N³⁻ → Ba₃N₂ (3(+2) + 2(-3) = 0).

image.caption: Ba₃N₂: barium nitride (charge balance determines 3:2 ratio).

classifications.value: Aluminum + nonmetal-ide (e.g., aluminum oxide, aluminum chloride)
characteristics: Ratios vary based on anion charge
classifications.behavior: Aluminum always +3, so naming is consistent; formula varies with anion charge.

examples.explanation: Al (aluminum) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: aluminum oxide. Formula: 2Al³⁺ + 3O²⁻ → Al₂O₃ (2(+3) + 3(-2) = 0).

image.caption: Al₂O₃: aluminum oxide (no Roman numeral; Al always +3).

examples.explanation: Al (aluminum) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: aluminum chloride. Formula: Al³⁺ + 3Cl⁻ → AlCl₃ (+3 + 3(-1) = 0).

image.caption: AlCl₃: aluminum chloride (1:3 ratio from charge balance).

examples.explanation: Al (aluminum) + N (nitrogen → nitride). Name: aluminum nitride. Formula: Al³⁺ + N³⁻ → AlN (+3 + (-3) = 0).

image.caption: AlN: aluminum nitride (1:1 ratio, charges balance directly).

classifications.type: Special Transition Metal Compounds (Zn, Ag, Cd)
classifications.value: Metal name + nonmetal-ide (e.g., zinc chloride, silver oxide)
classifications.behavior: These transition metals behave like Type I metals despite being in d-block; naming follows Type I pattern.

examples.explanation: Zn (zinc) + Cl (chlorine → chloride). Name: zinc chloride. Formula: Zn²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → ZnCl₂. Never 'zinc(II) chloride'.

image.caption: ZnCl₂: zinc chloride (Zn always +2, no Roman numeral).

examples.explanation: Ag (silver) + O (oxygen → oxide). Name: silver oxide. Formula: 2Ag⁺ + O²⁻ → Ag₂O. Never 'silver(I) oxide'.

image.caption: Ag₂O: silver oxide (Ag always +1, no Roman numeral).

examples.explanation: Cd (cadmium) + S (sulfur → sulfide). Name: cadmium sulfide. Formula: Cd²⁺ + S²⁻ → CdS. Never 'cadmium(II) sulfide'.

image.caption: CdS: cadmium sulfide (Cd always +2, no Roman numeral).

principles.statement: Nonmetal names change to end in -ide for binary ionic compounds: oxygen → oxide, chlorine → chloride, nitrogen → nitride, etc.
principles.application: Universal rule for all binary ionic compounds; memorize common nonmetal root changes.
principles.example: O → oxide, Cl → chloride, N → nitride, S → sulfide, P → phosphide, F → fluoride, Br → bromide, I → iodide.
principles.analogy: Like a uniform change: nonmetals put on their '-ide uniform' when in ionic compounds.

image.alt: Table of nonmetal names and their -ide forms
image.caption: Nonmetal → -ide transformation: oxygen→oxide, chlorine→chloride, etc.

principles.statement: Ionic compounds never use Greek prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, etc.); formula ratios are determined by charge balance, not prefixes.
principles.application: Distinguishes ionic from covalent naming: MgCl₂ is 'magnesium chloride' (not 'magnesium dichloride'); Na₂O is 'sodium oxide' (not 'disodium oxide').
principles.example: Correct: magnesium chloride (MgCl₂), sodium oxide (Na₂O). Incorrect: magnesium dichloride, disodium oxide.
principles.analogy: Like a marriage certificate: the names don't include numbers; the relationship (formula) is understood from the individuals (charges).

image.alt: Comparison showing correct vs. incorrect ionic naming with prefixes
image.caption: Ionic naming: never use prefixes; charge balance determines formula ratio.

principles.statement: From a Type I name, you can write the formula by: (1) Identify metal and its fixed charge, (2) Identify nonmetal and its charge, (3) Balance charges to zero.
principles.application: Enables formula writing from name: 'calcium nitride' → Ca²⁺ + N³⁻ → Ca₃N₂ (3(+2) + 2(-3) = 0).
principles.example: Name: aluminum sulfide → Al³⁺ + S²⁻ → Al₂S₃ (2(+3) + 3(-2) = 0). Name encodes all information needed for formula.

image.alt: Flowchart showing name-to-formula conversion for Type I compounds
image.caption: Name to formula: identify ions, balance charges, write formula.

components.definition: Type I naming pattern and nonmetal -ide suffixes
components.interpretation: Metal name unchanged (no Roman numeral); nonmetal root + -ide; common roots: ox-, chlor-, nitr-, sulf-, phosph-, fluor-, brom-, iod-.

worked_examples.title: Naming Type I Compounds from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following Type I ionic compounds: (a) KBr, (b) MgS, (c) AlN, (d) ZnO, (e) AgCl.

step_by_step.calculation: K = potassium (Group 1, Type I); Br = bromine → bromide. Name: potassium bromide.
Mg = magnesium (Group 2, Type I); S = sulfur → sulfide. Name: magnesium sulfide.
Al = aluminum (Group 13, Type I); N = nitrogen → nitride. Name: aluminum nitride.
Zn = zinc (special Type I); O = oxygen → oxide. Name: zinc oxide.
Ag = silver (special Type I); Cl = chlorine → chloride. Name: silver chloride.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) KBr: potassium bromide. (b) MgS: magnesium sulfide. (c) AlN: aluminum nitride. (d) ZnO: zinc oxide. (e) AgCl: silver chloride. All follow Type I pattern: metal name + nonmetal-ide, no Roman numerals, no prefixes.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming Type I ionic compounds from formulas using consistent pattern

worked_examples.title: Writing Formulas from Type I Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for the following Type I ionic compounds: (a) sodium phosphide, (b) calcium fluoride, (c) aluminum sulfide, (d) zinc nitride, (e) silver oxide.

step_by_step.calculation: Na⁺ (Group 1) + P³⁻ (phosphide) → Need 3 Na⁺ to balance 1 P³⁻ → Na₃P
Ca²⁺ (Group 2) + F⁻ (fluoride) → Need 2 F⁻ to balance 1 Ca²⁺ → CaF₂
Al³⁺ (Group 13) + S²⁻ (sulfide) → Need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 S²⁻ (-6) → Al₂S₃
Zn²⁺ (special) + N³⁻ (nitride) → Need 3 Zn²⁺ (+6) and 2 N³⁻ (-6) → Zn₃N₂
Ag⁺ (special) + O²⁻ (oxide) → Need 2 Ag⁺ to balance 1 O²⁻ → Ag₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Na₃P (3 Na⁺ + 1 P³⁻ = 0). (b) CaF₂ (1 Ca²⁺ + 2 F⁻ = 0). (c) Al₂S₃ (2 Al³⁺ + 3 S²⁻ = 0). (d) Zn₃N₂ (3 Zn²⁺ + 2 N³⁻ = 0). (e) Ag₂O (2 Ag⁺ + 1 O²⁻ = 0). All formulas from charge balance, no prefixes needed.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing Type I ionic formulas from names using charge balance

sections.section_title: Common Nonmetal Anions in Type I Compounds
sections.introduction: Type I ionic compounds pair fixed-charge metals with nonmetal anions. Knowing the common nonmetal anions and their charges is essential for naming and formula writing.

key_concept.definition: Common nonmetal anions in Type I compounds include: Group 15 (N³⁻ nitride, P³⁻ phosphide), Group 16 (O²⁻ oxide, S²⁻ sulfide), Group 17 (F⁻ fluoride, Cl⁻ chloride, Br⁻ bromide, I⁻ iodide).
key_concept.context: These anions have predictable charges based on their group: Group 15 gains 3 electrons (-3), Group 16 gains 2 electrons (-2), Group 17 gains 1 electron (-1). This predictability enables formula writing from names.

classifications.value: Always form -1 ions (F⁻, Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻)
characteristics: Gain 1 electron to achieve noble gas configuration
Most common anions in Type I compounds
classifications.behavior: All halogens form -1 ions in ionic compounds; charge is predictable from group position.

examples.explanation: F⁻ compounds: NaF (sodium fluoride), MgF₂ (magnesium fluoride), AlF₃ (aluminum fluoride). F always -1.

image.caption: Fluoride: always -1 charge in ionic compounds.

examples.explanation: Cl⁻ compounds: KCl (potassium chloride), CaCl₂ (calcium chloride), ZnCl₂ (zinc chloride). Cl always -1.

image.caption: Chloride: always -1 charge in ionic compounds.

characteristics: Gain 2 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
Common in metal oxides and sulfides
classifications.behavior: Oxygen and sulfur form -2 ions in ionic compounds; oxide is most common anion overall.

examples.explanation: O²⁻ compounds: Na₂O (sodium oxide), MgO (magnesium oxide), Al₂O₃ (aluminum oxide). O always -2.

image.caption: Oxide: always -2 charge in ionic compounds.

examples.explanation: S²⁻ compounds: K₂S (potassium sulfide), CaS (calcium sulfide), ZnS (zinc sulfide). S always -2.

image.caption: Sulfide: always -2 charge in ionic compounds.

characteristics: Gain 3 electrons to achieve noble gas configuration
classifications.behavior: Nitrogen and phosphorus form -3 ions in ionic compounds; nitrides and phosphides are less common but follow same rules.

examples.explanation: N³⁻ compounds: Li₃N (lithium nitride), Mg₃N₂ (magnesium nitride), AlN (aluminum nitride). N always -3.

image.caption: Nitride: always -3 charge in ionic compounds.

examples.explanation: P³⁻ compounds: Na₃P (sodium phosphide), Ca₃P₂ (calcium phosphide), AlP (aluminum phosphide). P always -3.

image.caption: Phosphide: always -3 charge in ionic compounds.

principles.statement: For nonmetal anions, charge = group number - 18: Group 17 = -1, Group 16 = -2, Group 15 = -3.
principles.application: Enables anion charge prediction without memorization; essential for formula writing from names.
principles.analogy: Like a countdown: Group 17 needs 1 more (→ -1), Group 16 needs 2 more (→ -2), Group 15 needs 3 more (→ -3).

image.alt: Periodic table showing anion charges by group
image.caption: Anion charges: Group 15 (-3), Group 16 (-2), Group 17 (-1).

principles.statement: All monatomic nonmetal anions end in -ide: oxide, sulfide, nitride, phosphide, fluoride, chloride, bromide, iodide.
principles.application: Recognizing -ide suffix identifies monatomic anion; distinguishes from polyatomic ions (which have -ate, -ite, etc.).
principles.example: Oxide (O²⁻), sulfide (S²⁻), nitride (N³⁻) are monatomic; sulfate (SO₄²⁻), nitrate (NO₃⁻) are polyatomic (different naming).

image.alt: Table of monatomic anion names with -ide suffix
image.caption: Monatomic anion naming: all end in -ide suffix.

components.definition: Common nonmetal anions and their charges
components.interpretation: Group 17: -1 (F⁻, Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻); Group 16: -2 (O²⁻, S²⁻); Group 15: -3 (N³⁻, P³⁻). All names end in -ide.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Anion Charges from Periodic Position
worked_examples.problem: Determine the charge and name for anions formed by: (a) fluorine, (b) sulfur, (c) nitrogen, (d) bromine, (e) phosphorus.

step_by_step.calculation: F is Group 17 → 17 - 18 = -1 → F⁻ → fluoride
S is Group 16 → 16 - 18 = -2 → S²⁻ → sulfide
N is Group 15 → 15 - 18 = -3 → N³⁻ → nitride
Br is Group 17 → 17 - 18 = -1 → Br⁻ → bromide
P is Group 15 → 15 - 18 = -3 → P³⁻ → phosphide

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) F⁻, fluoride (Group 17, -1). (b) S²⁻, sulfide (Group 16, -2). (c) N³⁻, nitride (Group 15, -3). (d) Br⁻, bromide (Group 17, -1). (e) P³⁻, phosphide (Group 15, -3). Anion charges predictable from group number; all names end in -ide.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting anion charges and names from periodic table position

worked_examples.title: Writing Formulas Using Anion Charges
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) potassium iodide, (b) magnesium oxide, (c) aluminum phosphide, (d) lithium sulfide, (e) calcium nitride.

step_by_step.calculation: K⁺ (Group 1) + I⁻ (Group 17) → K⁺ + I⁻ → KI (1:1, charges balance)
Mg²⁺ (Group 2) + O²⁻ (Group 16) → Mg²⁺ + O²⁻ → MgO (1:1, charges balance)
Al³⁺ (Group 13) + P³⁻ (Group 15) → Al³⁺ + P³⁻ → AlP (1:1, charges balance)
Li⁺ (Group 1) + S²⁻ (Group 16) → Need 2 Li⁺ to balance 1 S²⁻ → Li₂S
Ca²⁺ (Group 2) + N³⁻ (Group 15) → Need 3 Ca²⁺ (+6) and 2 N³⁻ (-6) → Ca₃N₂

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) KI (K⁺ + I⁻). (b) MgO (Mg²⁺ + O²⁻). (c) AlP (Al³⁺ + P³⁻). (d) Li₂S (2 Li⁺ + S²⁻). (e) Ca₃N₂ (3 Ca²⁺ + 2 N³⁻). All formulas from charge balance using predictable anion charges.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing Type I ionic formulas using predictable anion charges

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Even with simple Type I rules, students make predictable errors. Recognizing these pitfalls and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and confidence.

key_concept.definition: Common Type I nomenclature errors include: adding Roman numerals to fixed-charge metals, using prefixes in ionic naming, incorrect -ide suffixes, and charge balance mistakes in formula writing.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: Is the metal Type I? No Roman numeral? No prefixes? Correct -ide suffix? Charges balanced to zero?

classifications.type: Adding Roman Numerals to Type I Metals
classifications.value: Incorrectly using Roman numerals for fixed-charge metals
characteristics: Confusing Type I with Type II rules
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect names; Roman numerals only for variable-charge metals (Type II), never for Type I.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'sodium(I) chloride' for NaCl. Correct: 'sodium chloride'. Na is always +1; Roman numeral unnecessary and wrong.

image.alt: Comparison showing incorrect vs. correct Type I naming
image.caption: Type I error: never use Roman numerals for fixed-charge metals.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'aluminum(III) oxide' for Al₂O₃. Correct: 'aluminum oxide'. Al is always +3; Roman numeral unnecessary and wrong.

image.caption: Aluminum always +3: no Roman numeral needed.

classifications.value: Incorrectly applying covalent prefix rules to ionic compounds
characteristics: Confusing ionic with covalent naming rules
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect names; prefixes only for covalent compounds, never for ionic compounds.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'disodium oxide' for Na₂O. Correct: 'sodium oxide'. Ionic compounds don't use prefixes; charge balance determines ratio.

image.caption: Ionic naming error: never use prefixes; formula reflects charge balance.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'magnesium dichloride' for MgCl₂. Correct: 'magnesium chloride'. The 'di-' is implied by charge balance (Mg²⁺ needs 2 Cl⁻).

image.caption: MgCl₂: magnesium chloride (no prefix; 2 Cl⁻ required by charge balance).

classifications.value: Forgetting to change nonmetal name to -ide form
characteristics: Using element name instead of anion name
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect names; all binary ionic compounds require -ide suffix for the anion.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'sodium chlorine' for NaCl. Correct: 'sodium chloride'. Nonmetal must end in -ide in ionic compounds.

image.caption: Binary ionic naming: always change nonmetal to -ide form.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'calcium oxygen' for CaO. Correct: 'calcium oxide'. Oxygen becomes oxide in ionic compounds.

image.caption: Oxygen → oxide in ionic compounds; always use -ide suffix.

classifications.value: Writing incorrect formulas due to charge balance mistakes
characteristics: Not balancing total positive and negative charges to zero
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect formulas; total charge must equal zero for neutral ionic compound.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: NaO for sodium oxide. Correct: Na₂O. Na⁺ + O²⁻ needs 2 Na⁺ to balance: 2(+1) + (-2) = 0.

image.caption: Na₂O: 2 Na⁺ needed to balance 1 O²⁻.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: AlO for aluminum oxide. Correct: Al₂O₃. Al³⁺ + O²⁻ needs 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6): 2(+3) + 3(-2) = 0.

image.caption: Al₂O₃: 2 Al³⁺ (+6) balances 3 O²⁻ (-6).

principles.statement: Always verify Type I names by checking: (1) Metal is Type I (fixed charge), (2) No Roman numeral, (3) No prefixes, (4) Nonmetal ends in -ide, (5) Formula charges balance to zero.
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit.
principles.example: Name: magnesium chloride. Check: Mg is Type I ✓, no Roman numeral ✓, no prefix ✓, chloride ends in -ide ✓, Mg²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ = 0 ✓. All checks pass.
principles.analogy: Like a pre-flight checklist: verify each item before considering the name/formula correct.

image.caption: Type I checklist: fixed charge, no Roman numeral, no prefix, -ide suffix, charge balance.

principles.principle: Type I vs. Type II Distinction
principles.statement: Type I metals (fixed charge) never use Roman numerals; Type II metals (variable charge) always use Roman numerals. Correct identification is essential.
principles.application: Prevents mixing naming rules; Type I = Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd; Type II = most transition metals.
principles.example: NaCl: Na is Type I → sodium chloride (no Roman numeral). FeCl₂: Fe is Type II → iron(II) chloride (Roman numeral required).

image.alt: Comparison of Type I and Type II naming
image.caption: Type I vs. Type II: fixed charge (no Roman numeral) vs. variable charge (Roman numeral required).

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each name or formula and provide the correct version: (a) 'sodium(I) chloride' for NaCl, (b) 'disodium oxide' for Na₂O, (c) 'calcium chlorine' for CaCl₂, (d) AlO for aluminum oxide.

step_by_step.calculation: Error: Roman numeral for Type I metal. Correction: Na is always +1. Correct name: sodium chloride.
Error: prefix used for ionic compound. Correction: Ionic compounds don't use prefixes. Correct name: sodium oxide.
Error: missing -ide suffix. Correction: Nonmetal must end in -ide. Correct name: calcium chloride.
Error: charges don't balance. Al³⁺ + O²⁻ ≠ 0. Correction: Need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 O²⁻ (-6). Correct formula: Al₂O₃.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 'sodium(I) chloride' → sodium chloride (remove Roman numeral; Na always +1). (b) 'disodium oxide' → sodium oxide (remove prefix; ionic compounds don't use prefixes). (c) 'calcium chlorine' → calcium chloride (add -ide suffix). (d) AlO → Al₂O₃ (balance charges: 2 Al³⁺ + 3 O²⁻ = 0). Systematic verification catches these common Type I errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction using Type I verification checklist

sections.introduction: Nomenclature mastery requires active practice. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy with Type I ionic compound naming and formula writing.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: naming from formulas, writing formulas from names, identifying Type I metals, and correcting common errors.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce pattern recognition; focus on error patterns to target weak areas.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Type I Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Complete the following: (1-5) Name these compounds: NaF, MgO, AlCl₃, K₂S, Zn₃N₂. (6-10) Write formulas for: lithium bromide, calcium phosphide, silver oxide, aluminum sulfide, sodium nitride.

step_by_step.calculation: Na (sodium) + F (fluoride) → sodium chloride
Mg (magnesium) + O (oxide) → magnesium oxide
Al (aluminum) + Cl (chloride) → aluminum chloride
K (potassium) + S (sulfide) → potassium sulfide
Zn (zinc) + N (nitride) → zinc nitride
Li⁺ + Br⁻ → LiBr (1:1, charges balance)
Ca²⁺ + P³⁻ → Need 3 Ca²⁺ (+6) and 2 P³⁻ (-6) → Ca₃P₂
Ag⁺ + O²⁻ → Need 2 Ag⁺ to balance 1 O²⁻ → Ag₂O
Al³⁺ + S²⁻ → Need 2 Al³⁺ (+6) and 3 S²⁻ (-6) → Al₂S₃
Na⁺ + N³⁻ → Need 3 Na⁺ to balance 1 N³⁻ → Na₃N

worked_examples.final_answer: Names: (1) sodium fluoride, (2) magnesium oxide, (3) aluminum chloride, (4) potassium sulfide, (5) zinc nitride. Formulas: (6) LiBr, (7) Ca₃P₂, (8) Ag₂O, (9) Al₂S₃, (10) Na₃N. All follow Type I pattern: metal name + nonmetal-ide, no Roman numerals, no prefixes, charge balance determines formula.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive Type I nomenclature practice covering naming and formula writing

worked_examples.title: Type I Metal Identification Challenge
worked_examples.problem: For each compound, identify if the metal is Type I. If Type I, name the compound. If not Type I, write 'Type II - needs Roman numeral': (a) NaCl, (b) FeCl₂, (c) MgO, (d) CuO, (e) Al₂O₃, (f) ZnS, (g) AgCl, (h) PbO.

step_by_step.calculation: Na is Group 1 → Type I → sodium chloride
Fe is transition metal (not Zn, Ag, Cd) → Type II - needs Roman numeral
Mg is Group 2 → Type I → magnesium oxide
Cu is transition metal (not Zn, Ag, Cd) → Type II - needs Roman numeral
Al is Group 13 → Type I → aluminum oxide
Zn is special fixed-charge → Type I → zinc sulfide
Ag is special fixed-charge → Type I → silver chloride
Pb is post-transition metal with variable charge → Type II - needs Roman numeral

worked_examples.final_answer: Type I (name): (a) NaCl → sodium chloride, (c) MgO → magnesium oxide, (e) Al₂O₃ → aluminum oxide, (f) ZnS → zinc sulfide, (g) AgCl → silver chloride. Type II (needs Roman numeral): (b) FeCl₂, (d) CuO, (h) PbO. Type I metals: Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd only.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Distinguishing Type I from Type II metals for correct nomenclature approach

summary: Type I binary ionic compounds contain fixed-charge metals (Groups 1, 2, Al, Zn, Ag, Cd) and nonmetals. Naming pattern: [metal name] [nonmetal root]-ide, with NO Roman numerals and NO prefixes. Type I metals: Group 1 (+1), Group 2 (+2), Al (+3), Zn (+2), Ag (+1), Cd (+2). Nonmetal anions: Group 17 (-1, -ide), Group 16 (-2, -ide), Group 15 (-3, -ide). Formulas determined by charge balance (total positive = total negative = 0), not prefixes. Examples: NaCl (sodium chloride), MgO (magnesium oxide), Al₂O₃ (aluminum oxide), ZnCl₂ (zinc chloride), Ag₂O (silver oxide). Common errors: adding Roman numerals to Type I metals (wrong), using prefixes in ionic naming (wrong), missing -ide suffix (wrong), charge balance mistakes in formulas (wrong). Verification checklist: Type I metal? No Roman numeral? No prefix? -ide suffix? Charges balance? Practice distinguishes Type I (fixed charge, no Roman numeral) from Type II (variable charge, Roman numeral required). Mastery of Type I naming is foundation for all ionic nomenclature.
